steps.text: Verify the final fuel location, review respective records, and discuss with personnel involved in the most recent fuel movement activity.
Determine if the final location of the fuel is consistent with that specified in the licensee's records.

preconditions.text: Direct observation of a fuel movement activity is not possible at the time of inspection.

constraints.text: Approved procedures must be used in accordance with TS and security plan requirements.
Ensure fuel is not damaged, accidental criticality does not occur, and radiation and contamination hazards are minimized.
Failure of a fuel element can be dramatic (e.g., TRIGA element blister cracking), causing high air particulate alarms, evacuation, and substantial water/airborne radioactivity.
Handle fuel in accordance with TS and applicable procedures during refueling, fuel shuffling, and fuel inspection.
Participants must be trained, understand their tasks, and perform dry runs (including accident mitigation discussions) for operations of potential safety concern or unusual nature.
Fuel handling tools must be consistent with TS requirements and licensee procedures.

steps.text: Transfer fuel to storage pits, racks, or containers designed for the purpose, or remove only one bundle at a time to be inspected underwater and then replace before proceeding.
Monitor changes in the level of neutron flux when changing the core configuration.
Perform 1/M calculations independently by more than one person.
Inspect fuel and control rods in accordance with the TS and procedures to visually determine fuel integrity.
Check the control rods to determine their capability to continue to function.
Investigate and resolve control rod failures to ensure they perform their intended function to shut down the reactor.
Inspect the removed neutron source for leakage paths such as splitting or pitting.

preconditions.text: If an installed neutron source is removed during refueling

constraints.text: The threat of inadvertent criticality varies; four bundles in a square matrix pose a criticality hazard.
Some vane-type control blades might fall out of the core if broken.
